purpose: Audit log for GF parsing repairs considered for original US Constitution main-text fragments. Entries here are not automatically applied by the witness exporter. A correction becomes trusted only after the exporter consumes it, regenerates witnesses, and Lean conformance builds.

unknownTokenPolicy.note: ParseEng does not provide a safe generic unknown lexical item in this lane. Words such as "someone" parse as ordinary lexical content, so they must not be used as placeholders for unknown constitutional actors.

corrections.originalText: The Senate shall have the sole Power to try all Impeachments

observedFailure.reason: ParseEng produces a typed skeleton with a PGF meta hole in the controlled infinitive.

corrections.recommendedHandling: Split into exact subfragment plus contextual action completion, or represent the whole legal construction in a custom Constitution grammar.

candidateFixes.parserInput: The Senate shall have the sole Power
candidateFixes.justification: Exact contiguous subfragment of the original clause; ParseEng parses it without meta holes.

candidateFixes.parserInput: The Senate shall try all Impeachments
candidateFixes.justification: The controlled infinitive 'to try all Impeachments' is governed by the subject 'The Senate' in the same clause. This preserves the actor/action relation but drops the explicit 'sole Power' modality.
candidateFixes.source: Original Article I, Section 3 wording; tested with ParseEng and no meta hole.

candidateFixes.parserInput: The Senate shall have the sole Power to try all Impeachments
candidateFixes.justification: The exact legal construction should ultimately be typed as a power-bearing controlled-infinitive clause rather than split into two ordinary English sentences.
candidateFixes.source: Observed ParseEng hole; legal syntax of Article I, Section 3.

observedFailure.reason: ParseEng treats the infinitive fragment as a phrase with an implicit subject and inserts a PGF meta hole.

corrections.recommendedHandling: Use a Constitution-specific grammar category for enumerated powers. Treat any ordinary-sentence completion as contextual and weaker than exact surface grounding.

candidateFixes.justification: Article I, Section 8 introduces Congress's powers, and 'To declare War' is one item in that list. 'May' captures power better than duty, but it is not the literal modal text.
candidateFixes.source: Article I, Section 8 power-list context; tested with ParseEng and no meta hole.

candidateFixes.justification: This parses without meta holes and supplies the governing actor, but it risks converting a granted power into a duty. It should not be used for legal semantics except as a syntactic smoke test.
candidateFixes.source: Article I, Section 8 power-list context; tested with ParseEng and no meta hole.

candidateFixes.justification: The exact fragment is not a standalone sentence; it is an enumerated PowerItem under 'The Congress shall have Power'. A custom grammar can type it directly without inventing a subject or modal.
candidateFixes.source: Original Article I, Section 8 wording and observed ParseEng meta hole.

corrections.originalText: shall propose Amendments to this Constitution

observedFailure.reason: ParseEng produces a verb-phrase sentence witness with a missing subject before 'shall'.

corrections.recommendedHandling: Context-complete with 'The Congress' only when the Article V antecedent is tracked separately; otherwise use a custom grammar for conditional duties.

candidateFixes.parserInput: The Congress shall propose Amendments to this Constitution
candidateFixes.justification: The subject 'The Congress' is explicit in the same Article V sentence. This completion parses without meta holes, but omits the condition 'whenever two thirds of both Houses shall deem it necessary'.
candidateFixes.source: Original Article V wording; tested with ParseEng and no meta hole.

candidateFixes.parserInput: shall propose Amendments to this Constitution
candidateFixes.justification: The exact fragment is a conditional legal duty whose subject and antecedent are supplied by the surrounding sentence. A custom grammar should preserve those dependencies instead of flattening them.
candidateFixes.source: Original Article V wording and observed ParseEng meta hole.
